--- SOURCE 2: Advancing Management Theory through Interdisciplinary ... ---
URL: https://journals.aom.org/doi/10.5465/amj.2024.4006

SUMMARY:
# Advancing Management Theory through Interdisciplinary Research: Challenges and Opportunities However, in our experience as editors, we have observed several common challenges that authors face when conducting interdisciplinary research, which often limit their ability to provide novel and meaningful insights into management phenomena. Further, interdisciplinary research may appear fragmented or unnecessarily complex if it lacks demonstration of how the integration of external theories provides added value or advances the field of management. For interdisciplinary research to make a strong theoretical contribution (and meet the third challenge), scholars must move beyond simply applying theories from one discipline to another. By navigating these challenges thoughtfully, scholars can produce interdisciplinary work that not only expands management theory but also positions the field to make impactful contributions in a time of rapid and fundamental technological, societal, and organizational transformation.

From the EditorsFree Access

# Advancing Management Theory through Interdisciplinary Research: Challenges and Opportunities

Published Online:9 Dec 2024<https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.4006>

Interdisciplinary research in management has the potential to drive novel insights and foster impactful, problem-driven research. As management scholarship increasingly engages with transformative societal challenges—such as sustainability, artificial intelligence (AI), and digital transformation—interdisciplinary research can provide a source of theoretical and methodological innovation, as well as a promising approach for addressing these complex, pressing issues of our time ([Shaw, Bansal & Gruber, 2017](#b16); [Shaw, Tangirala, Vissa & Rodell, 2018](#b17)). Simultaneously, however, interdisciplinary research comes with its own challenges. Congruent with the overall mission of the journal, articles published in the *Academy of Management Journal* (AMJ) must clearly advance theory—more specifically, management theory. Thus, interdisciplinary scholarship must go beyond the mere application of knowledge or theories from other disciplines; it must also advance theory in its own right. Relatedly, there needs to be clear added value for the management field, bringing together separate bodies of knowledge. Theories developed from interdisciplinary research thus must have greater explanatory power than those based on a single discipline, shed light on unexplored factors, or help us see a phenomenon in a novel way. This can only be achieved through theoretical depth; hence, disciplinary expertise and competence are generally a foundation for successful interdisciplinary work ([Sieldok & Hibbert, 2014](#b18)).

The purpose of this editorial is to encourage scholars to consider interdisciplinary research within their own research portfolios, and help them identify and overcome some of the inherent challenges associated with it. Beyond drawing from prior work on the topic, as well as our own experiences, we informed our work by reaching out to more than a dozen management scholars who have successfully conducted interdisciplinary research projects. We solicited their insights on key elements characterizing not only the process of conducting interdisciplinary research but also their motivations leading to, and the outcomes resulting from, such research.[1](#fn1) Below, we describe what we mean by interdisciplinary research, present pertinent challenges scholars may face, and provide recommendations for addressing them.

## DEFINING INTERDISCIPLINARY RESEARCH

Interdisciplinary research has been, and continues to be, an important focus of interest, discussion, and study. Scholars from multiple domains and disciplines are interested in understanding what interdisciplinary research entails and how to conduct it. As a basis, it is important to first define interdisciplinarity and analytically distinguish it from multidisciplinarity and transdisciplinarity, its two “neighboring” concepts. Interdisciplinarity can be thought of as

> *a process of answering a question, solving a problem, or addressing a topic that is too broad or complex to be dealt with adequately by a single discipline or profession…[interdisciplinary research] draws on disciplinary perspectives and integrates their insights into a more comprehensive perspective. ([Boon & Van Baanen, 2019](#b3): 16)*

There is broad consensus that the primary driver of interdisciplinarity is the inherent complexity of the problem or phenomenon at hand—whether it involves a problem in science or a practical “real-world” problem ([Boon & Van Baanen, 2019](#b3); [Cheng, Henisz, Roth & Swaminathan, 2009](#b5); [Okhuysen & Bonardi, 2011](#b14)).

Interdisciplinarity research “resides” between multidisciplinarity and transdisciplinarity research on a continuum depicting the level of integration between the source disciplines and the extent to which the resulting outcome differs from those original disciplines. Multidisciplinarity “can be conceptualized as involving two or more divergent disciplines, which approach a research problem by using their own framings and methods, at a level that requires cooperation rather than integrative collaboration” while “transdisciplinarity can be defined as the development of increasing coherence, unity, and simplicity of knowledge in which disciplinary boundaries become irrelevant or are radically reshaped” ([Sieldok & Hibbert, 2014](#b18): 198). An alternative approach for differentiating between these three types of research is to consider some commonly associated terms: integrating, linking, and focusing in interdisciplinarity research; juxtaposing, sequencing, and coordinating in multidisciplinarity research, and transcending and transforming in transdisciplinarity research ([Klein, 2010](#b12): 16).

## THE VALUE OF INTERDISCIPLINARY RESEARCH

The mission for the 23rd editorial AMJ team has been to develop scholarly research for better understanding the “Transformational Times for Management and Organizations” in which we live, including widespread AI deployment, digitalization of work, and complex societal challenges ([Gruber, 2023](#b8)). Over the years, management scholarship has made significant strides, becoming more pluralistic and open to diverse geographical contexts, questions, and methods ([Van Baalen & Karsten, 2012](#b19)). It has positioned itself at the forefront of societal transformations, aiming to develop impactful management theories that address grand challenges and complex societal problems. Yet, there remains much potential for interdisciplinary research to develop more innovative and creative theories for this transformational era.

### Theoretical Innovation

First, interdisciplinary research can provide a source of theoretical novelty and thereby drive theory building. Breakthrough innovation often comes from bringing together more distant types of knowledge and diverse inputs ([Gruber, Harhoff & Hoisl, 2013](#b10); [Hargadon, 1998](#b11)). Management research has a rich history of borrowing frameworks and ideas from other disciplines in transformative ways, such as the adoption of ideas from biology for developing population ecology theory constructs from geology to understand group compositional dynamics, or theories of complexity from physics. Today, fields like engineering, the humanities, law, and neuroscience can provide new tools, perspectives, and theories that inform management theories. As one of the respondents we surveyed eloquently noted:

> *It seems to me that there’s much to be gained by looking beyond our disciplinary boundaries, partly because (like travel) it “broadens the mind,” but also because one often finds that scholars elsewhere have wrestled with understanding similar phenomena (if perhaps within a different language game) and may well be ahead of us down the runway (or remind us of how high the bar really is).*

In this way, interdisciplinary research expands theoretical horizons, having the potential to offer a richer and more nuanced understanding of the phenomenon or problem.

### Methodological Advances

Second, interdisciplinary methodological advances in areas such as machine learning ([von Krogh, Roberson & Gruber, 2023](#b20)), computational modeling, latent mixture modeling, and qualitative comparative analysis ([Bliese, Certo, Smith, Wang & Gruber, 2024](#b2)) can offer innovative ways to explore new management issues or help us see existing issues in novel ways. Several of our prior editorials have called for an expansion of our methodological tool set to offer new avenues for theory development and testing ([Gruber & Bliese, 2024](#b9); [Langley, Bell, Bliese, LeBaron & Gruber, 2023](#b13)). While integrating methodologies can be challenging—as one of our surveyed respondents shared: “The statistical methods were very different … and that took some time to learn how to explain to ‘our’ community how they work”—we believe the effort can be worthwhile.

### Problem-Driven Research

Third, interdisciplinary scholarship is uniquely positioned to advance problem-driven research that seeks to address urgent and complex societal problems, which require new theoretical lenses ([Ravasi, Zhu, Wan, Dorobantu & Gruber, 2024](#b15); [Shaw et al., 2018](#b17)). For instance, understanding an organization’s transition to net zero is increasingly challenging without simultaneously understanding the complexities of nature, society, and business. Hence, in-depth knowledge about climate policy, as well as the science of climate change, is necessary in this regard. By integrating diverse sources of knowledge, perspectives, and methods, interdisciplinary research can enable scholars to generate breakthroughs that would be difficult to achieve within the confines of a single discipline. As one of our surveyed authors noted:

> *My motivation for engaging in interdisciplinary research was that I needed to be able to solve problems that could not be solved by using one research paradigm. It happened organically by working with economists, but it also happened strategically by seeking to work with computer scientists, electrical engineers, medical doctors, and epidemiologists.*

By integrating diverse perspectives, theories, and methods, interdisciplinary research can lead to breakthrough solutions that would be difficult to achieve within a single discipline.

However, interdisciplinary research can also be inherently challenging. A discipline typically defines the epistemic boundaries (boundaries drawn around knowledge and methods) but also social boundaries of a particular intellectual community. An interdisciplinary researcher is, by definition, transgressing and spanning these boundaries. As one respondent noted, interdisciplinary research may require some trial and error and adaptability: “We tried to present our early papers in different conferences and different divisions of the Academy of Management. Our initial ‘bets’ were all wrong.” We therefore provide some guidelines for authors to avoid common pitfalls and realize the potential of interdisciplinarity.

## COMMON CHALLENGES IN INTERDISCIPLINARY SUBMISSIONS

In the prior section, we explored ways in which interdisciplinary research can contribute to management science. However, in our experience as editors, we have observed several common challenges that authors face when conducting interdisciplinary research, which often limit their ability to provide novel and meaningful insights into management phenomena. We discuss the most predominant of these challenges below, with the goal of guiding authors toward designing and conducting high-quality interdisciplinary research that supports AMJ’s mission.

### Challenge 1: Lack of Clear Motivation

The first common challenge is providing a compelling rationale for incorporating theories from other fields into management and organization studies. We have noticed that this often stems from limited explanations for why existing management theories and frameworks are inadequate to understand the phenomena being studied. If the limitations of existing management theory are not explicitly addressed, questions about the relevance and applicability of the imported conceptual frameworks may arise, as it becomes unclear whether such lenses are essential for revealing novel insights or understanding previously unexplored phenomena. In other words, why are these theories essential? This may be the case, for instance, when another discipline has developed relevant insights into a phenomenon that cannot be overlooked, as captured by a respondent who stated: “It simply didn’t make sense to review and compile what management scholars know about our topic without addressing what economists have said.”

Further, interdisciplinary research may appear fragmented or unnecessarily complex if it lacks demonstration of how the integration of external theories provides added value or advances the field of management. Thus, clear articulation of the motivation for interdisciplinary approaches is critical for ensuring that the research is able to offer meaningful new insights. In disciplinary management studies, the articulation of a theoretical motivation is imperative—and often challenging. Authors need to demonstrate how existing theoretical frameworks inadequately explain or predict phenomena. Interdisciplinary management studies have to demonstrate these disciplinary challenges too, and they must show how the import and integration of theory from other fields resolve these challenges. It is thus no small feat to resolve this “double challenge.” As one respondent stated, “The main challenge is that reviewers are often critical of arguments/theories that come from outside our discipline. Thus, it is much harder to convince them of their value.”

### Challenge 2: Absence of a Common Language

Another frequent challenge in interdisciplinary research is the lack of shared semantics, syntax, and pragmatics, which can create obstacles for authors, reviewers, and editors ([Carlile, 2004](#b4)). When authors rely on novel concepts or frameworks from other disciplines, they may struggle to convey their ideas clearly to readers within the discipline. Additionally, introducing jargon specific to other fields can be unfamiliar or confusing for reviewers accustomed to established terminology within management. Reviewers may be reluctant to spend considerable time attempting to understand new terminology and specific uses of concepts. Reviewers may also lack patience if authors offer terse definitions and implicitly ask the reviewers to go back to the source literature to understand novel concepts. The linguistic disconnect between the source and target disciplines can obscure the manuscript’s contributions, making it harder for reviewers to assess the validity and relevance of the research. One of our respondents noted this challenge with regard to methods by observing that they had engaged in “lots of ‘translation’ activities. You need to explain methods (over and over again) and ‘educate’ all the time.”

### Challenge 3: Insufficient Theorizing

We have also observed a lack of theoretical depth in interdisciplinary submissions, which often stems from a failure to fully integrate theories from different fields. Although authors may draw on multiple disciplines to provide new insights or explanations, insufficient grounding in these disciplines may lead to superficial or inaccurate knowledge. One respondent succinctly summarized this point by observing that “Even reviewers from your own discipline will recognize generalizations, mischaracterizations, shortcuts, and dabbling.” Similarly, integrative attempts can fall short when the theories remain separate and disjointed. Instead of forming a cohesive conceptual framework, theories across disciplines are often presented without sufficient development of the connections between them. As a result, the use of imported theory is more illustrative, symbolic, or metaphorical, representing the application of a novel perspective rather than offering nuanced insights into the phenomenon of interest. While a novel perspective on a management phenomenon has great merits ([Cornelissen, Oswick, Christensen & Phillips, 2008](#b6)), papers published in AMJ demand additional theoretical integration to make a sufficiently strong theoretical contribution.

It is also important to note that the challenges in interdisciplinary theorizing may be magnified by the distance between the component fields, where authors and readers share little ontological or epistemological ground. For example, theorizing across management and physics would be particularly challenging given divergent underlying assumptions, methodologies, and ontological and epistemological foundations (e.g., [Barad, 2007](#b1)). Nevertheless, we also want to emphasize that theorizing across closely related disciplines may prove just as challenging because fields that use seemingly similar terminology may be substantively different. Accordingly, a careful and comprehensive integration of theories across disciplines can enhance the richness and explanatory power of research.

### Challenge 4: Practical Considerations

The authors we surveyed also identified specific practical challenges that arose while they were conducting their interdisciplinary research. First, they noted the significant amount of time that interdisciplinary work takes. As one respondent pointed out, “it often means reading more and reading more widely, making it less efficient.” Another agreed that there is a “need to read very deeply” while also understanding “where the acceptable boundaries of the field are.” Additionally, when papers incorporate methodologies from multiple disciplines, it becomes necessary to “learn both methods” and “get everyone involved in the publication process on board with the theory and methods.” This complexity can lead to another frustration—the difficulty of “having too few pages to do justice to complex conversations in other disciplines.” Another challenge relates to the reception of interdisciplinary work.

## RECOMMENDATIONS

Recognizing the importance of these challenges in interdisciplinary research, we offer several recommendations to help overcome them. By implementing these strategies, researchers may better align theoretical frameworks and methodologies to create more cohesive and theoretically impactful studies. Addressing these challenges not only strengthens the research but also fosters greater collaboration and innovation across fields.

### Clearly Articulate the Motivation for Interdisciplinary Research

To meet the first challenge—the lack of a clear rationale for integrating theories from other fields into management studies—we recommend that authors provide a strong motivation for why interdisciplinary approaches are not only useful but also necessary. This involves answering two crucial questions: (1) Why are existing management theories inadequate to explain the phenomenon? and (2) How does an interdisciplinary approach provide a solution that advances the field? The integration of external theories should not add mere complexity for its own sake. Instead, it should be evident that these borrowed perspectives are indispensable to understanding the phenomenon at hand. In other words, interdisciplinary theories should be “must haves” rather than merely “nice to haves,” and their positioning needs to be carefully considered ([Dencker, Gruber, Miller, Rouse & von Krogh, 2023](#b7)).

### Bridge the Language Gap between Disciplines

To address the second challenge—the lack of a shared language between different disciplines—we recommend that authors engage in “translation work,” carefully explaining the key concepts and terminologies they draw from other fields while demonstrating their value to the management field. Authors must take great care to guide the reviewers in understanding the origin of novel concepts and how these offer a new way to study a phenomenon in management. This linguistic bridging ensures that interdisciplinary research remains accessible and comprehensible to management scholars, reviewers, and a wider audience without alienating those who have responsibility for evaluating its merit. Similarly, when sourcing methods from other fields, authors should carefully explain the new methods they are using as well as why they are being used, explaining their merit, origins, and philosophical assumptions. This demonstrates that the borrowed methods are methodologically sound and helps reviewers understand the relevance and rigor of these methods. Such translation efforts are needed to allow reviewers to appreciate the rigor and relevance of interdisciplinary approaches, ensuring the work is not dismissed due to unfamiliarity with the concepts or techniques borrowed.

However, when research paradigms are truly incommensurable (i.e., when they operate on fundamentally different assumptions about reality [ontology], knowledge and methods [epistemology], and even values and norms [ethics]), authors should be transparent about these distinct paradigmatic underpinnings rather than attempting to force incompatible perspectives into alignment. Authors can still serve as translators between different schools of thought, but must even more carefully explain how concepts or methods from one paradigm relate to the phenomenon of interest in another. For instance, they can explain how each paradigm speaks to different dimensions of the phenomenon by juxtaposing insights from each approach. By acknowledging disciplinary incommensurability and clearly specifying which insights come from each perspective, authors can offer a multifaceted view that adds value without forcing artificial alignment.

### Demonstrate Depth of Interdisciplinary Theorizing

For interdisciplinary research to make a strong theoretical contribution (and meet the third challenge), scholars must move beyond simply applying theories from one discipline to another. Certainly, borrowing from other disciplines can be a very useful strategy for advancing problem-driven research and arriving at a more comprehensive and multidimensional understanding of management phenomena. However, applying theory from one discipline to another by itself does not typically result in the creation of new theory, and all too often leads to fragmented or disjointed frameworks. Unless disciplinary borrowing is used to achieve creative recombinations of knowledge in the process, it may fall short of AMJ’s mission to contribute to advancing management theory.

To move beyond surface-level borrowing, scholars may engage in “hybridization” or “synergizing,” in which theories from different fields are combined to address complex problems that cannot be effectively tackled within a single discipline (see also [Sieldok & Hibbert, 2014](#b18)). Hybridization means that borrowed tools or approaches become integral components of theories of the recipient discipline. Disciplinary knowledge is thereby recombined in creative ways, allowing scholars to develop new ways of solving problems within management scholarship. One example is how machine learning tools developed in computer science and mathematics help management scholars to uncover novel patterns in their data and make predictions ([Shrestha, He, Puranam & von Krogh, 2021](#b50)). Through synergizing, scholars more deeply integrate approaches from two or more disciplines ([Sieldok & Hibbert, 2014](#b18)). This can be a very powerful way of developing new theories. When such contrasting perspectives offer complementary insights, researchers can develop a more comprehensive and complete explanation of a phenomenon. In turn, when contrasting perspectives have conflicting assumptions (e.g., sociological versus economic explanations for behavior), scholars may use their research as an opportunity to devise a new explanation that synthesizes insights from both fields. This approach, which transcends individual disciplines, represents one of the deepest forms of integration achievable in a single published article.

In sum, it is not enough to simply transplant concepts from another discipline; authors must show how their interdisciplinary approach advances theorizing. Rather than using external theories in an illustrative or symbolic way, we recommend that authors focus on combining theories in a way that enhances their explanatory power.

### Theoretical Fidelity

A final challenge in interdisciplinary research is ensuring that theories, methods, and tools borrowed from other fields are used with the necessary truthfulness, fidelity, and rigor as in their original context. Scholars must demonstrate a deep engagement with the source literature to avoid superficial or erroneous use of concepts. This requires more than superficial borrowing; it requires respecting the integrity of the original methods or theories while thoughtfully adapting them to new management contexts. Consulting with experts from the source discipline—a practice that several of our survey respondents used—can be another important step to ensure that borrowed theoretical constructs are applied accurately and precisely. This practice enhances both the validity and credibility of interdisciplinary work, ultimately strengthening its quality.

## CONCLUDING THOUGHTS

In conclusion, interdisciplinary research holds tremendous potential for deepening our understanding of complex management phenomena and fostering theoretical and methodological innovation. Yet, as explored in this editorial, the pursuit of interdisciplinary research in management comes with several notable challenges—such as the need for a clear and compelling rationale, difficulty of achieving a shared language and conceptual framework, and requirement for depth in theorizing. By navigating these challenges thoughtfully, scholars can produce interdisciplinary work that not only expands management theory but also positions the field to make impactful contributions in a time of rapid and fundamental technological, societal, and organizational transformation.

Interdisciplinary research challenges our traditional boundaries, offering new avenues for understanding complex phenomena that cannot be fully captured by a single discipline. Realizing the potential of interdisciplinary research in management will depend not only on scholars’ ability to integrate diverse insights but also on the academic community’s collective commitment to supporting and rigorously evaluating such work. This requires a scholarly environment that supports transformative contributions by balancing depth and rigor with openness to innovative interdisciplinary ideas. Reviewers play a pivotal role as gatekeepers, and to fairly evaluate interdisciplinary contributions, they must be equipped to appreciate theories and methods from multiple fields and be willing to cross disciplinary boundaries. We, as editors, also recognize our responsibility to ensure that interdisciplinary submissions are evaluated thoroughly, with a reviewer pool that is well-versed in diverse domains.

In sum, in a world where problems are rarely confined to a single discipline, interdisciplinary research is essential to generate transformative insights and make impactful contributions. It thus holds much potential for advancing the mission of AMJ to enrich and advance management theory.

1 We are grateful for their insights and feedback, as they not only helped us in carving out several key themes but also provided illustrative quotes regarding the benefits from and challenges in conducting interdisciplinary research.

# [Removing Obstacles to Interdisciplinary Population Health Research](https://iaphs.org/removing-obstacles-interdisciplinary-population-health-research/)

One of the most exciting things about being a population health researcher is the opportunity to collaborate across disciplinary boundaries to address health problems requiring multiple perspectives and approaches. For example, an article in [*Nature*](https://www.nature.com/news/interdisciplinary-research-by-the-numbers-1.18349) in 2015 highlighted interdisciplinary trends in publication and showed their increasing relevance in health research as well as the potential for the author’s growth and influence when publishing outside of their own discipline.

While the call for interdisciplinarity is welcome, one of the biggest challenges is determining how to effectively collaborate across overlapping but frequently acrimonious or incongruous fields.  And while some research institutes and universities have reorganized around priority areas, most of us spend our time researching and writing within fairly traditional boundaries with conventional and discipline-based assessment processes. So the question is, *how do we move beyond simply calling our research interdisciplinary to actually doing interdisciplinary research* *?*

Here are some suggestions I’ve found helpful.

***Learn to speak across disciplines.*** Communication is one of the biggest challenges to interdisciplinary research. Even within my Health Policy and Management department, where everyone has similar health-research goals, unique definitions and jargon persist due to discipline-specific training (e.g., economics, sociology, health policy). We frequently have different preferences in methodological approaches and struggle with terminology. This becomes even more pronounced when working with individuals from the broader health care sector, where I have discovered that seemingly basic terms such as *population health* mean different things to researchers, policy makers, health insurers, or health information specialists. Familiarity with the language and jargon of a particular field comes from frequent communication and contact. Attendance at research seminars and participation in working groups that span these divides are ways to enhance communication, familiarity, and trust.[![](https://iaphs.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/05/Tal-pop-health-header-300x219.jpg)](https://iaphs.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/05/Tal-pop-health-header.jpg)

![](https://iaphs.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/05/Tal-pop-health-header-300x219.jpg)

***Establish and maintain relationships early and often.*** Another way to overcome the barrier of communication is through established trust. I find this particularly hard as a social scientist working within a school of medicine, where there are established hierarchies of the disciplines. Social scientists are frequently brought onto teams as an afterthought, for program implementation or to meet grant criteria asking for interdisciplinary teams. Part of this problem can be traced to a lack of awareness of respective disciplines’ technical capabilities.  For example, while the skills of biostatisticians are often established and well-known, I frequently have to define what a demographer is, and I continue to learn about and appreciate the skills of local policy makers. Cross-department, community, or research-themed meet-and-greets or presentations are one way to advertise skills as well as potential collaborative and data opportunities. Trust relies on full integration into teams and is contingent on time investment and the ability to promote our work and skills.

***Encourage early-career investigators.*** It is important to incentivize early-stage interdisciplinary research sooner rather than later. Yet besides a few successful training programs, there are strong disincentives to do so in university settings. The structural and logistical challenges are challenging to overcome and add risk to the already stressful process of tenure and promotion. Negotiating job expectations with department chairs and directors is particularly important for junior faculty members and those in joint-positions across departments or research institutes. Current barriers to interdisciplinary research include a number of value judgements (varying by discipline, position, and department) that have implications for tenure or promotion decisions. These include types of research (e.g., applied vs. basic science); types of collaboration (e.g., university vs. community groups); appropriate dissemination outlets; authorship expectations (e.g., number of collaborators; authorship order); and grant funding streams (e.g., foundations vs. federal).  Transparency and clear job expectations regarding these barriers are required to achieve success. In addition, a strong network of like-minded researchers in organizations such as IAPHS can make change by communicating about and encouraging interdisciplinary work through mentorship and as external promotion letter writers.[![](https://iaphs.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/trees-showing-two-heads-cropped-300x239.jpg)](https://iaphs.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/trees-showing-two-heads-cropped-e1528240335176.jpg)

![](https://iaphs.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/trees-showing-two-heads-cropped-300x239.jpg)

***Encouraging collaborations.*** There are structural opportunities to overcome some of the barriers I’ve listed. For one, graduate training offers a unique opportunity to build interdisciplinary research. I have found that serving as a mentor or committee member can encourage graduate students and postdocs to practice interdisciplinary research, appreciate various skill sets, and to integrate into broader research and policy communities. Moreover, respect and trust are often built among mentors over the course of interdisciplinary mentoring and training.

Also, universities need to take more ownership for encouraging collaborations. While universities increasingly promote specific initiatives that emphasize the importance of interdisciplinary research and collaboration, they don’t often dedicate the necessary resources. Small steps such as supporting cross-discipline talks or workshops through innovative grant programs that incorporate both the research and local community can go a long way in influencing a university culture. Serious efforts to integrate researchers often require both physical space and dedicated support for the research time required to establish trust and a shared vocabulary. Universities need to recognize that interdisciplinary research is crucial for science and deserves the substantial investment required.

As IAPHS members are best placed to understand, the biggest health problems today require approaches that draw on methods, theories, and resources from multiple areas of social, behavioral, policy, and medical inquiry.  If we are to make strides, we need to consistently work to reduce structural barriers to interdisciplinarity, incentivize and reward collaboration, and present opportunities that maintain communication and build the trust necessary to avoid the temptation to work in our traditional silos.

# **The Challenges and Benefits of Interdisciplinary Research: Crossing Boundaries**

In today’s rapidly evolving world, the boundaries between academic disciplines are becoming increasingly porous. Traditional silos of knowledge that once separated fields like science, humanities, and social sciences are giving way to a new era of interdisciplinary research. Interdisciplinary research, which involves collaboration and the blending of insights from multiple disciplines, is gaining prominence as scholars recognize the need to address complex and multifaceted problems that defy easy categorization. While interdisciplinary research holds great promise for advancing our understanding of the world, it also comes with its own set of challenges and benefits.

### **Benefits of Interdisciplinary Research**

### **Challenges of Interdisciplinary Research**

### **Strategies for Successful Interdisciplinary Research**

Overcoming the challenges of interdisciplinary research requires careful planning and execution. Here are some strategies to promote successful interdisciplinary collaboration:

### **Examples of Successful Interdisciplinary Research**

Several examples of interdisciplinary research have yielded significant contributions to society:

### **Conclusion**

Interdisciplinary research holds tremendous potential for addressing the multifaceted challenges of our world. While it comes with its own set of challenges, the benefits of interdisciplinary collaboration cannot be overstated. By fostering effective communication, providing necessary resources, and recognizing the contributions of interdisciplinary researchers, we can harness the power of collaboration across boundaries to drive innovation and solve complex problems. In an era where the boundaries between knowledge domains are becoming increasingly blurred, embracing interdisciplinary research is not just an option but a necessity for advancing our understanding of the world and improving the human condition.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Disciplinary barriers manifest as rigid boundaries within academia that hinder collaboration, leading to fragmented research and limiting the potential for innovative solutions across fields.
Data compatibility issues arise when integrating datasets from various disciplines, often due to differing formats, standards, and methodologies, complicating the synthesis of comprehensive insights.

The need for training and resources is crucial for researchers to effectively navigate interdisciplinary methodologies, requiring investment in educational programs and collaborative tools for success.
